tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to learn what Orbit Wars is, how Hydra-first training is invoked with `uv run python -m src.train`, and how responsibility groups under `conf/` shape experiments. This document is the map for the rest of the tour: JAX training path, campaign outputs, and W&B sweeps.

tour.title: Agent Repository Guide
tour.description: AGENTS.md complements the README with repository-specific conventions: where Python runtime code lives, which pytest commands validate changes, and how Hydra config groups relate to `src/conf_schema.py` and training entrypoints. Treat it as the operational handbook for anyone modifying training or tests.

tour.title: Hydra Training Entry
tour.description: The canonical training entrypoint is a thin Hydra wrapper: it resolves CLI overrides into a validated `TrainConfig` and immediately delegates to the JAX training orchestrator. Following this file answers the question "what actually runs when I call `python -m src.train`?"
tour.languageLesson: Hydra's `@hydra.main` decorator binds CLI overrides to a config directory; the entrypoint should stay thin so composition and validation live in dedicated config modules.

tour.title: Config Schema & Runtime
tour.description: Building on the entrypoint, the reorganized `src/config/` package defines canonical dataclass responsibility groups and composes Hydra/OmegaConf YAML into validated runtime objects. The schema is the contract; the runtime module is where orbit rules, feature dimensions, and training knobs are enforced before JAX sees them.
tour.languageLesson: Dataclass-based config schemas in Python give you typed defaults and validation at compose time, which is critical when JAX JIT shapes depend on fields like player count, candidate count, and feature history length.

tour.title: Hydra Config Groups
tour.description: YAML under `conf/` is the source of truth for experiments: the root config composes model, task, reward, training, opponents, curriculum, telemetry, and artifacts groups. Pair the root file with a representative model preset and training defaults to see how CLI overrides like `model=attention` map to runnable jobs.
tour.languageLesson: Hydra config groups use `defaults:` lists to compose YAML trees; overriding with `group=key=value` on the CLI swaps entire responsibility slices without editing Python.

tour.title: JAX Training Orchestrator
tour.description: Once `TrainConfig` is built, `src/jax/train.py` owns the full loop: vectorized rollouts, PPO updates, curriculum stage transitions, telemetry emission, and checkpoint persistence. This is the hub that imports env, features, policy, and PPO modules discovered in the next steps.

tour.title: Game Simulation Layer
tour.description: Before JAX vectorization, the Python game package defines shared constants, typed state/actions, and the reference environment implementing reset/step, opponent sampling, and terminal rewards. Understanding this layer explains what the JAX env must stay aligned with for parity tests to pass.

tour.title: Feature Encoding Pipeline
tour.description: Observations flow through an ordered feature registry that fixes tensor layouts, then through Python encoders with history buffers and turn snapshots. The JAX feature module mirrors that layout so policy inputs stay checkpoint-compatible across Python and JAX paths.

tour.title: Vectorized RL Stack
tour.description: The JAX stack turns composed config into batched simulation and learning: the env kernel handles reset/step and rollout grouping, the policy module hosts Flax backbones and pointer decoders, and PPO wires rollouts to GAE and clipped surrogate updates. Together these three files are the performance-critical training path.
tour.languageLesson: JAX training code is shape-sensitive: changing feature dimensions or player counts in config alters JIT compilation and checkpoint compatibility, so env, features, and policy must agree on the same tensor layout.

tour.title: Curriculum & Opponents
tour.description: Training quality depends on who the agent plays against and how stages advance. The curriculum controller tracks promotion metrics and stage views, the opponent pool supplies curriculum-aware weight vectors, and trajectory shielding guards invalid planet/ship collisions during rollouts in both Python and JAX helpers.

tour.title: Telemetry & Metrics
tour.description: After rollouts and PPO updates produce scalars, the metric registry defines which signals are canonical and how Hydra toggles metric groups, while the logger binds those records to Weights & Biases and local JSONL paths. This layer is how you debug throughput regressions and stage promotions without reading raw arrays.

tour.title: Verification & Packaging
tour.description: The project stays safe to refactor because pytest locks config composition and Python/JAX parity, while `pyproject.toml` declares the UV-managed dependency groups used by CI and local dev. Run these tests after touching schema, env, or feature dimensions to catch silent shape or semantics drift early.
tour.languageLesson: `uv` reads `pyproject.toml` dependency groups (for example `dev`) so `uv run --group dev pytest` reproduces the same toolchain agents and CI use without a separate requirements file.

tour.title: OMG MCP Workflow Server
tour.description: Alongside training code, the TypeScript MCP server exposes OMG workflow tools—state, PRD, memory, checkpoints, bridge imports, and ultragoal tracking—for agent orchestration in Cursor. The package entry registers tool groups; it is a separate deployable from the Python RL runtime but shares the repo's agent workflow story.
